manualPages.label: Tool 01 · Sale To Be Made
manualPages.title: A Sale To Be Made.
manualPages.text: Part One · Day-0 Playbook A New Rep's First Ninety Days Tool 01 · Sale To Be Made A Sale To Be Made. ENTR-454 / T01 Sale To Be Made Protocol A 03 / 30 Purpose. Before the math, before the script, before the ladder — answer six questions out loud. What you sell, to whom, in what steps, how long, what you fear, and what changes if you do this well. If any answer is fuzzy, the week is already behind. A The Six Prompts — Answered Out Loud Say These Before You Touch The Phone, The Door, or The Keyboard 01 / What What is my sale to be made? One clearly dosed energy-plus-hydration stick for crews that already buy energy drinks — traded for a real-shift trial and an honest yes/no. 02 / Who To whom? P1 responders & military-adjacent sponsors · P2 gym / combat / responder-fitness · P3 healthcare, label-first . 03 / Steps Six key steps to the sale Name the room — earn the intro — ship a coded micro-trial — crew tries on a real shift — honest yes/no — route: re-order, station bag, or clean exit. 04 / How Long Typical time to close 3–8 weeks from first named contact to first paid re-order. Responder / MWR longer; coach / gym shortest (sometimes one WOD). 05 / Fear Biggest fear Shipping a kit into a room that never tries it — budget spent, no signal back. Second: a healthcare pitch that overclaims and burns the lane. 06 / Upside If I do this well A named crew, coach, or unit becomes a repeat buyer + community reference. One station referral unlocks the next two. Peer adoption, not polish, moves the book. B Activity Ladder — Weekly Minimums Floor, Not Ceiling 01 · Mon Build list 25 named buyers. Gym, station, MRF or retail. Tier P1/P2/P3. ► 02 · Mon–Fri Reach out 115 touches. Walk-in, call, DM, email. In-person × 3. ► 03 · Tue–Fri Qualify 23 qualified convos. Four-Gear in first 4 minutes. ► 04 · Fri Trial / route 5 coded micro-trials shipped. Honest follow-up date per. Friday Scoreboard · Numbers Only Convos Qualified Trials Yes/No Back 115 / ▢ 23 / ▢ 5 / ▢ 3 / ▢ Miss the floor Monday, week's behind. Report the miss — don't hide it. C Revenue Math Sidebar Because "How Long" Has To Match A Number $42k Rep Quarterly Goal 13 Working Weeks $84 Wholesale / Case 22% Trial → Re-Order Therefore Need ~38 paid cases per quarter → ~170 qualified convos → ~58 coded trials shipped → 5 trials / week is the floor that prices the goal. Illustrative planning assumption — confirm current wholesale, conversion, and quota figures with manager before citing. See APX G. Rule / T01 — Six answers first. Activity second. Math prices the time. 01 What · 02 Who · 03 Steps · 04 Time · 05 Fear · 06 Upside

manualPages.label: Tool 02 · Vitamins & Painkillers · Part A
manualPages.title: Vitamins & Painkillers.
manualPages.text: Tool 02 · Vitamins & Painkillers · Part A Vitamins & Painkillers. ENTR-454 / T02-A Worksheet Protocol B.1 04 / 30 Purpose. A six-prompt worksheet you answer out loud before you open your mouth. Know what you sell, who for, its value, how you tailor to a vitamin buyer vs. a painkiller buyer, and exactly where Restless is each. Label-first, evidence-first, no overclaim. A What Do You Sell? One Sentence, Concrete A portable energy-and-hydration stick for long shifts — 120 mg caffeine, 240 mg L-theanine, 1,000 mg electrolytes in one serving. 120 mg Caffeine 240 mg L-Theanine 1k mg Electrolytes Don't say: wellness, biohack, nootropic, supplement stack. Do say: energy + hydration, one stick, long shifts. B Who Is Your Target Customer? Three Tiers, Priority Order P1 · Primary First responder stations & military-adjacent sponsors who can approve or route a micro-trial. P2 · Secondary Gym, combat sports, first-responder-fitness adjacencies — coach-first, member-second. P3 · Tertiary Healthcare shift-work buyers — label-first, evidence-first, no overclaim. C What Is Your Product's Value? The One Job We Actually Do Shift workers get one clearly dosed option instead of stacking coffee plus a separate hydration mix. We do not claim stronger, medical, clinical, or only-in-category. We do claim: one stick, one label, every dose visible. Functional / Social / Emotional Functional. Moderate caffeine + electrolytes in one wrapper. Social. A product the crew / coach / unit is okay being seen with. Emotional. "This one was made for my shift." F Where Is Restless A Vitamin? Where Is It A Painkiller? By Segment · Know Before You Dial Segment Read Why (In The Buyer's Words) What To Offer First CFD / EMS station Painkiller "Crew is stacking coffee and Monster on overnights — captain doesn't love it." 5–10 coded sticks, one overnight, honest yes/no from crew. Responder-fitness gym (555) Painkiller "Members drag in the back half; fitness lead wants something the crew will actually use." Coach-only 5-stick kit. Test after the WOD, not during. MRF / veteran endurance Painkiller "Training days are long, ruck water gets old fast." 3–5 coded sticks to a named athlete. Honest feedback before wider seeding. MWR / base gym Mixed "Interested, but need a label and a small trial first." Label PDF first, then 5–10 coded sticks to fitness lead. CrossFit / hybrid gym Mixed "Coach will try it. Owner cares about shelf margin." Coach-first 5 sticks. No retail talk until coach says yes. BJJ / MMA gym Mixed "Head coach decides. Don't interrupt class." Talk after training. Coach-only 3–5 sticks, zero member-facing pitch. Supplement shop Vitamin "Another energy-hydration SKU in a crowded shelf." Skip unless they pull us in. No shelf push before P1/P2 trials close. Healthcare shift-worker Vitamin "Interested — but compliance asks for evidence." Label & evidence review first. No kit until they ask. Rule / T02-A — Six prompts answered out loud. Vitamin rooms get narrower; painkiller rooms get a coded trial. 01 What · 02 Who · 03 Value · 04 Vit · 05 Pain · 06 Where

manualPages.label: Tool 02 · Message Architecture · Part B
manualPages.title: Call Router & From–To Lines.
manualPages.text: Tool 02 · Message Architecture · Part B Call Router & From–To Lines. ENTR-454 / T02-B Call Router Protocol B.2 05 / 30 Purpose. When the buyer talks, route. When the buyer resists, reframe — from their frame, to ours. Every line below is field-tested; the goal is a trial, not a pitch. A From → To Statements Reframe The Conversation · Then Say The Rep Line Situation From (Their Frame) To (Our Frame) Rep Line — Say It Out Loud Responder names 3 AM fatigue Another can when the back half gets rough A real-shift test of one clearly dosed stick "That back-half moment is exactly where I'd test it. Let the crew try it on a real overnight and tell us if it earns a spot." Responder: crew buys their own Department budget objection Crew-choice trial, no budget ask "Then I'm not asking for department budget. Can I send a small station trial and let the crew decide?" Military buyer, supplement-skeptical Unknown powder from a new brand Exact label, every dose visible, review before trial "I'd rather send the label first and let you cross-check it than ask you to take our word for it." Gym buyer loves incumbent brand Brand fight Comparison trial without trashing incumbent "If what you use is working, I'm not here to argue. This is a lower-dose, exact-label option to compare." Healthcare asks for evidence Free-sample pitch Evidence-first review "For healthcare, I'd rather send the label and evidence summary first than pretend this is a floor-sample conversation." Buyer says "send me info" Lost in inbox Specific artifact + permissioned follow-up "Absolutely. Do you want the short version, the label, or both? And what follow-up, if any, is okay?" B Call Router — First 60 Seconds Pick Path On Buyer Response Heats Up "Keep going." — Restate their pain in their words. Name the 3 AM moment. Offer a station trial. Not Me "Not my problem." — Fire one specific 3 AM symptom. Still no — thank, exit, 90-day re-queue. Price First "How much?" — Answer cleanly. Pivot to crew-buys-their-own economics. Do not apologize. Label Ask "Let me see the label." — Send it first. No pitch. Supplement Facts, exact doses, honest on NSF. Incumbent "I use [X]." — Ask what's working. Do not attack. Offer side-by-side in their shop. Brush-Off "Send me info." — Ask which artifact (short, label, or both) and what follow-up is okay. Set a date. C Progress Made · Pain Reduced Tight Lines, Per Segment Segment Tight Line To Say First responder "This is not a wellness initiative. It's a small station-table trial for crews already buying their own energy drinks." Military / MWR "Start with the current label and visible dose information. Review it first, then decide if a stick trial makes sense." Gym / combat "Not trying to replace pre-workout. Lighter, exact-label option for people who want energy plus hydration." Healthcare "For healthcare, the value is label-first review — not a hype trial." Rule — one segment, one line, one ask. Never mix segments in a single call. Rule / T02-B — Reframe from their world to ours. End with a trial, not a pitch. 01 Hear · 02 Reframe · 03 Route · 04 Trial

manualPages.label: Tool 03 · Walk & Talk
manualPages.title: Walk & Talk.
manualPages.text: Tool 03 · Walk & Talk Walk & Talk. ENTR-454 / T03 Walk & Talk Protocol C 06 / 30 Purpose. Four rehearsed openings for four real moments: a 30-second when you have the room's ear, a 15-second when you don't, a Sales Trailer when a peer asks what you do, and a catchphrase + brand line for the rest of it. Rehearsed out loud, not read. A Two Lengths, One Spine — Say Them Out Loud Who It's For – What Breaks – What We Do – The One Ask B / 30 Seconds The full pitch "Crews working overnight shifts stack coffee and Liquid I.V. to stay sharp — two products, two wrappers, no dose they can verify. Restless is one stick, one label : 120 mg caffeine, L-theanine, and electrolytes. Can I send a coded five-stick trial for the crew to test on one real overnight?" Use When You have the room's full attention — captain's office, coach after class, MWR fitness desk, event booth handoff. C / 15 Seconds The short pass "One stick for long shifts — caffeine, L-theanine, electrolytes on one label . Want me to send a small coded trial so your crew can test it on a real night?" Use When Chance encounter, quick walk-by, crew on the way out. No slide, no card. Plant the line and the ask. F Catchphrase / Hashtag Primary Sales Phrase "Built for the back half." Use in elevator opens, email subjects, voicemail closes, field events. Hashtag (Social Only) #BuiltForTheBackHalf G Brand Line Not The Default Sales Hook Stay in the Fight. Broader brand line. Packaging, apparel, station signage — not the opener on a cold call. Deploy it after a crew has tried the product, never before. When To Use Each "Built for the back half." — selling moments. "Stay in the Fight." — brand moments. H Do · Don't Tells Of Rehearsal vs. Improv Do Rehearse out loud, 5× before your first live pitch. Lead with the buyer's crew, not your product. End every version with a coded-trial ask. Don't Open with title, founding year, or investors. Use "premium," "clean," "innovative" — all vitamins. Close with "does that make sense?" Kills the ask. D Sales Trailer — When A Peer Asks What You Do "I sell Restless — one stick for the back half of a long shift. Crews already buying their own energy drinks try it on a real overnight and tell us if it earns a spot." No pitch · no ask · one line they'll remember. The Spine — Coaching Note Unchanging across all lengths: who it's for – what breaks – what we do – the one ask. If you skip the spine, you're improvising. Rule / T03 — 30 seconds. 15 seconds. Trailer. Catchphrase. One ask, always a coded trial. 30s Full · 15s Short · Tr Trailer · CP Catchphrase

manualPages.label: Tool 04 · Network Discipline
manualPages.title: Network Every Week · Where To Show Up.
manualPages.text: Tool 04 · Network Discipline Network Every Week · Where To Show Up. ENTR-454 / T04 Network Discipline Protocol D 07 / 30 Purpose. A 5-day rhythm with one gear per day, anchored to a field map of 14 communities where Restless already has standing. Each community carries a named person, the prep that earns respect, and a single coded ask. No cold decks. No unnamed ships. A Execution · The Five Gears — One Spin Each, Mon–Fri Miss A Gear 2 Weeks Running — Flag To Manager Gear 01 / Mon · Target 3 Old Warm 3 dormant contacts, > 6 months. Text, one line, no ask. Gear 02 / Tue · Target 2 New Warm 2 recent intros within 72h. Attach one useful thing, not a pitch. Gear 03 / Wed · Target 5 Cold In Named, not listed. Researched by hand. LinkedIn, local news, Instagram. Gear 04 / Thu · Target 1 Give First One intro, share, or thank-you. Zero asks this day. Gear 05 / Fri · Target 1 In Person Event, walk-in, or ride-along. Counts 3× a call. B Event & Community Roster — Where To Show Up First 14 Places · Named People · Exact Asks Tier Legend P1 Responder / military-adjacent P2 Gym / combat / adjacencies P3 Supplement shops, CPG, industrial Tier Place / Community Who To Meet Prep Exact Ask P1 Marine Recon Foundation · 0321 Day / Recon Challenge Recon alumni, organizers Know the Daniel / MRF relationship. Coded stick packs ready. "Who should receive the next small trial kit for honest feedback?" P1 Semper Fi / America's Fund · Century Hike Hikers, veteran connectors 3–5 coded stick packs. "Can I send a trial kit to people training for the hike?" P1 MURPH events · New Guy Prep · host gyms Host coach, veteran athletes Know the mission. Do not public-pitch the event. "Can coaches test a small kit after the WOD?" P1 555 Fitness -affiliated firehouse gyms Fitness lead + captain Identify fitness lead and captain before outreach. "5–10 coded sticks for the crew — one question: did they use them?" P1 CFD / EMS stations, charity stair climbs Captain, lieutenant, organizer Respect kitchen-table culture. Stay brief. "Can I drop a small station pack for the crew to try on shift?" P1 IAFF / local fire union meetings Union officer, benefits lead Know local chapter & wellness agenda. "Who handles member wellness or station trial products?" P1 MWR / base gyms (e.g. Great Lakes) MWR fitness manager Label PDF & trial kit ready before the call. "Should I send the label first, or mail a small kit to the fitness lead?" P2 Bunker Labs / veteran founder events Veteran founders, operators Prepare founder story and two intro asks. "Who in your network already reaches military or first responders?" P2 CrossFit / hybrid gyms near Chicago Owner, GM, head coach Look for retail / demos. Coach-first ask. "Who decides what coaches can sample or recommend?" P2 BJJ / MMA gyms and open mats Owner, head coach, fight-team lead Do not interrupt class. Talk after training. "Can I leave a coach-only kit before any member-facing pitch?" P2 Local race / charity events (5K, ruck, hero WOD) Event organizer, fitness sponsor lead Bring kit + signage; do not headline. "Can we hand a coded kit to the captain of each team?" P3 Supplement shops · local independents Owner / buyer Only after P1/P2 trial proof. Bring label first. "Can I bring back proof from station trials before any shelf talk?" P3 CPG meetups / Chicago beverage scene Operators, distributors Learn shelf math; never pitch as a retail product first. "Who else is selling shift-worker first, retail second?" P3 Industrial / trade safety meetings Safety officer, site supervisor Know the shift profile. No claims around safety. "Would a small site trial after a long shift be useful to you?" Rule / T04 — Five gears per week. Ten rooms. Named people. One ask each. No cold decks. 01 Five Gears · 02 Know The Room · 03 Name The Person · 04 Bring One Ask

manualPages.label: Tool 05 · Bullseye & First 5
manualPages.title: Bullseye Targets.
manualPages.text: Tool 05 · Bullseye & First 5 Bullseye Targets. ENTR-454 / T05 Bullseye & First 5 Protocol E 08 / 30 Purpose. Stop prospecting "the world." Name the center of the bullseye (one room, one person), name the first five contacts by the end of this week, and name the disqualifier that keeps you out of the wrong rooms. Vague pipelines die; specific ones ship sticks. A The Bullseye — Where Restless Wins First Name The Center. Everything Else Is Ring 3 Or Further. R1 FIRE+MRF R4 · Healthcare / Shops R3 · CF / BJJ / MMA R2 · 555 + MWR R1 Responder station & MRF / veteran endurance. Named captain or fitness lead, crew already stacking coffee + Monster on overnights, able to receive a 5–10 stick coded trial this week. Goal — 5 coded trials this quarter R2 555-Fitness firehouses & base MWR. Indirect path — warm intro required. Label-first, then ask if a trial is welcome. R3 CrossFit / BJJ / MMA & combat sports. Coach-first only. No member-facing pitch. R4 Healthcare shift work & supplement shops. Do not prospect first. Earn in via Ring 1–3 proof. B The First Five — Name Them This Week No Title-Only Entries # Named Human · Role Room / Community This Week's Move 1 — · Captain / Fitness Lead CFD Station 18 (R1) 5-stick coded trial · text Mon AM 2 — · MRF Coach MRF Recon Challenge (R1) 3-stick coach pack · in-person Fri 3 — · Captain / Union Rep IAFF Local · CFD (R1) Label PDF + 1 station ask 4 — · Fitness Manager Great Lakes MWR (R2) Label first · trial ask Thu 5 — · Head Coach / Owner 555 Fitness gym (R2) Coach-only 5 sticks · in-person Rule. A name without a room is a wish. A room without a name is a pipeline ghost. Both fields full by Friday or it's not on the board. C Disqualifier — Stay Out Of The Wrong Room Cheap "No" Beats Expensive "Maybe" No named human after one warm intro — walk away, re-queue 90 days. Crew does not already use coffee + an energy drink — not a painkiller room. Buyer wants a retail / shelf talk before a station trial — defer to R4 path. Healthcare wants a hype trial — label & evidence first or honest exit. Decision window > 6 months with no event trigger — re-queue, do not push. D Ring Scorecard — Refresh Every Friday Where Your Week Actually Went Ring Segment Named Contacts Coded Trials Shipped Honest Yes/No Back Re-Orders Started Weekly Target R1 Fire + MRF + responder — — — — 5 / 3 / 2 / 1 R2 555 + MWR + base gym — — — — 3 / 2 / 1 / — R3 CF / BJJ / MMA / hybrid — — — — 3 / 1 / 1 / — R4 Healthcare + shops (earn-in) — — — — 1 label send max Rule. Every shipped trial is under a unique Shopify code generated before the package leaves. No code, no ship. Rule / T05 — Name the center. Name the five humans. Ship only with a code. 01 Bullseye · 02 First 5 · 03 Coded Trial · 04 Honest Y/N

manualPages.label: Tool 06 · Competitive Map
manualPages.title: What They Already Drink.
manualPages.text: Tool 06 · Competitive Map What They Already Drink. ENTR-454 / T06 Competitive Map Protocol F 09 / 30 Purpose. Restless is never the first drink a buyer picks up. Know what is — coffee, energy cans, electrolyte mixes — and hold one clean seat on the shelf next to them. Compare, don't attack. The buyer almost certainly knows someone at the other brand. A The Shelf — Restless vs. What They Actually Use Energy Cans · Electrolyte Mixes · The "Free Option" Axis Restless Coffee Free Option Monster Celsius Ghost / C4 Liquid I.V. LMNT What it is Form factor Caffeine + hydration stick. One mix, water bottle. Station pot or kiosk cup. 16 oz can, 160 mg caffeine. 12 oz can, 200 mg, "fitness". Pre-workout & cans, 200–300 mg. Hydration stick, 500 mg Na, no caffeine. Hydration stick, 1,000 mg Na, no caffeine. Why crews use it Real reason One mix for the 3am slump — not two. Free, social, kitchen-table. Habit. 2 AM caffeine spike. "Lighter feel" vs. Monster. Pre-workout ritual, gym habit. Hangover & heat hydration. Keto / sodium-forward crowd. Restless compared Honest read Energy & hydration in one stick; moderate caffeine with L-theanine. We don't replace it. We sit next to the pot. Lower caffeine, no can, no dye. No "lite" positioning. Plain label. Not a pre-workout. Not a stimulant stack. Same format — ours has caffeine + L-theanine. Same format — ours has caffeine + fewer electrolytes. What we concede Never spin Newer brand. Not NSF certified yet. Not mass-shelved. Free. Nothing beats free. Brand recall + distribution. Marketing spend & gym-retail shelf. Pre-workout ritual ownership. Hydration-science recall. Electrolyte-science purism. Rule. Do not claim stronger, cleaner, healthier, safer, or only-in-category. Claim one job : energy + hydration in one stick, at a dose a responder can actually defend. B When The Buyer Names One Say This · Stop Talking vs. Coffee "Coffee works. We're not trying to replace the pot. The question is whether a stick in a water bottle helps on the second half of a 24." vs. Monster / C4 / Ghost "We're lower caffeine and no can. If a few of the crew are buying those on their own, they may want to test a stick instead — I'd rather let them decide." vs. Celsius "Celsius is a strong can with a fitness halo. We're a stick, not a can. Different routine. Different doses. Same kind of user — your crew." vs. Liquid I.V. "Liquid I.V. is hydration without caffeine. If someone's already using it and still reaches for coffee, that's two products — we're one." vs. LMNT "LMNT owns the sodium-forward crowd. Different audience. If a buyer's already there, we don't argue — we ask what's missing on caffeine days." C Shelf Rules Concede · Compare · Never Attack Walk the aisle first. Name every SKU before you say one of ours. Concede one thing out loud. Not certified yet, not mass-shelved yet. Buyers trust reps who don't pretend. Compare on one axis. Caffeine dose, form factor, OR hydration. Never all three at once. Never attack an incumbent. The buyer probably knows someone there. Offer side-by-side, not swap. "Let the shelf decide, not the rep." If buyer won't drop coffee, don't argue. Ask: "Where does coffee fall short — if anywhere?" No health claims to beat a competitor. Ever. If All Else Fails. Drop the comparison. Ask: "What would you need to see in a small trial to even consider a second option?" Listen, code the trial, follow up by the date you set. Rule / T06 — Know the shelf. Concede one thing. Compare on one axis. Never attack. 01 Shelf · 02 Concede · 03 Compare · 04 Side-By-Side

manualPages.label: Tool 07 · Qualification Protocol
manualPages.title: Who You're Talking To.
manualPages.text: Tool 07 · Qualification Protocol Who You're Talking To. ENTR-454 / T07 Personas Protocol G 10 / 30 Purpose. Four real Restless buyer archetypes — Brandon, Marcus, Christina, Derek. Know which one is in front of you by the second sentence. Qualify by the fourth minute. If any gear fails, exit politely and move on — do not push. A Four-Gear Qualification — First Four Minutes One Gear Per Minute · One Question Each Gear 01 · :60 Named Pain Is there a painkiller they'll name? "Where does the current routine fall short?" ► Gear 02 · :60 Access Can they approve or route a small coded trial? "Who decides what the crew tries?" ► Gear 03 · :60 Trial Fit Does a 5–10 stick coded kit make sense here? "If I sent a small trial, where would it be tested?" ► Gear 04 · :60 Feedback Loop Will you get an honest yes/no? "Who will tell me if it flopped?" Four "yes" in four minutes — ship a coded trial. Any "no" — thank, exit, flag the gear that failed. Code is generated before the box leaves. B Brandon Persona 01 · Responder Crew / MRF Athlete Who Firefighter, medic, or vet-endurance athlete. Mid-30s. Runs MURPH, works 24s, already buys his own Monster. Opening Ask 10-stick coded trial for one overnight. Honest crew yes/no by next shift. C Marcus Persona 02 · CF / Hybrid Gym Owner Who Owner-operator. Runs 4am and 5:30pm classes, cares about shelf margin, watches member retention like a hawk. Opening Ask Coach-only 5-stick kit. If coaches say yes — conversation about a small retail trial, not a case. D Christina Persona 03 · 555 Fitness Lead / MWR Manager Who Station fitness lead or MWR fitness manager. Early 30s. Label-literate, trust-gated, protects her crew. Opening Ask Label PDF, then — only if invited — a 5–10 stick coded trial for her crew. E Derek Persona 04 · BJJ / MMA Head Coach Who Head coach / academy owner. Often vet-owned. Trust is earned at the mat, not in email. Opening Ask Coach-only 3-stick kit. Zero member-facing pitch. Honest yes/no back in 10 days. F Exit Lines — When A Gear Fails Leave Clean · Re-Queue Later Gear 01 fails (no named pain) — "Not a fit today. Can I check back next quarter if anything changes?" Gear 02 fails (no access) — "Would a 10-minute call with [decider] help, or is this not the moment?" Gear 03 fails (no trial fit) — "Sounds like even a small trial is premature. I'd rather send the label and circle back." Gear 04 fails (no feedback) — "I need an honest yes/no — without it I can't ship. Is there someone who can be that point?" G Disqualifiers — Walk Away Don't Waste The Quarter Buyer can't name a pain after 2 minutes. No named decision-maker after 2 asks. Wants a coded trial with no feedback loop. Decision window > 6 months, no event trigger. Ring 4 contact with zero R1–R3 proof behind you. Repeated "send info" with no date. Rule / T07 — Brandon / Marcus / Christina / Derek — name them before you pitch them. 01 Named Pain · 02 Access · 03 Trial Fit · 04 Feedback

manualPages.label: Tool 08 · Prospecting Script
manualPages.title: Prospecting Script.
manualPages.text: Tool 08 · Prospecting Script Prospecting Script. ENTR-454 / T08 Prospecting Protocol H 11 / 30 Purpose. A word-for-word opening, four segment-specific meeting asks , the five objections you'll hear in week one, and how to get past gatekeepers and admins without lying about why you're calling. Read it out loud five times, then throw it away. A The Opening — 45 Seconds Cold Call, Walk-In, Or Voicemail :00 "Hi [name], this is [your name] with Restless. I know you didn't expect my call — I'll be quick and I don't need a decision today." :10 "I work with [named peer in their segment] on a simple thing: energy and hydration in one stick for long shifts. 120 mg caffeine, L-theanine, electrolytes — nothing fancy." :25 "Two questions — does your crew hit a wall on the back half of a shift or class? And who would decide whether they try something new? " B The Meeting Ask — By Segment Never The Same Ask Everywhere Segment / Persona The Ask What You Ship (Code Required) The One Question Back R1 · Fire / EMS station Brandon / Captain One overnight, crew test, no budget ask. 10-stick coded pack to station kitchen. "Did the crew keep it or chuck it?" R1 · MRF / veteran endurance Brandon / Named Athlete Training-block trial, one athlete, one honest read. 5 coded sticks to the athlete, not the org. "Did you use them, and would a buddy want some?" R1–R2 · 555 / MWR Christina Label review first; crew trial only if invited. Label PDF + Supplement Facts. Kit later, only on request. "Anything in the label that blocks a small trial?" R2 · CF / hybrid gym Marcus Coach-only 5-stick kit. No member-facing pitch. 5 coded sticks to head coach, hand-off. "Did the coaches use them in their own training?" R3 · BJJ / MMA Derek Coach-only 3-stick kit. After training, not before. 3 coded sticks to head coach, no member talk. "Honest read: keep talking or close the door?" C Gatekeeper · Admin · Assistant Tell The Truth · Earn The Routing Be honest about why. "I'm a sales rep — I have a small trial question for [name]." Ask the routing question. "Who handles wellness trials or crew samples here?" Offer the artifact, not the meeting. "Would a one-page label be easier to pass along?" Always thank by name. Admins remember reps who didn't bullshit them. D Five Objections, Week One Handle In Under 20 Seconds "Send me info." — Which artifact? What follow-up is okay? Set a date. "No budget." — Then no budget ask. Crew-buys-their-own trial. "We use [X]." — Side-by-side trial. Don't attack. "Not interested." — "Fair. Anything that would make a 5-stick kit worth a try?" "Talk to compliance." — Label first. No kit until they ask. E Voicemail Template Under 20 Seconds "Hi [name] — [your name] with Restless. Quick question: does your crew hit a wall on the back half? If yes, I have a small coded trial idea — no invoice, just one honest yes/no from the crew. If no, ignore this. I'll try Thursday at 2. [Number, said twice.] " Rule — one question, one number, one callback time. Never "just checking in." F Follow-Up Cadence No-Reply Pattern Day Channel Message D+1 Email Breakthrough email (see T09). D+3 Phone One call. Voicemail if no answer. D+7 In-person Drop label + 1 coded stick. No pitch. D+14 Text One line: "Worth a 4-min walk-in?" D+30 Exit Release to 90-day re-queue. Rule / T08 — Segment the ask. Respect the gatekeeper. Never ship without a code. 01 Open · 02 Ask (Seg) · 03 Gatekeeper · 04 Code & Ship

manualPages.label: Tool 09 · Written Outreach
manualPages.title: Breakthrough Email & Fact Bank.
manualPages.text: Tool 09 · Written Outreach Breakthrough Email & Fact Bank. ENTR-454 / T09 Written Outreach Protocol I 12 / 30 Purpose. One phone screen. One question. One ask. No attachments unless asked. Below the email: a durable fact bank of what we actually know — formulation, label, trial mechanics — so no rep has to invent numbers in the moment. A The Email — One Phone Screen Default Variant · R3 Gym Owner (Marcus) To marcus@ironworksgym.com From rep@drinkrestless.com Subject Would your coaches try something new on the back half? Marcus — Short note, no attachment. We make Restless — one stick, energy + hydration for long sessions (120 mg caffeine, L-theanine, electrolytes). Built to sit next to the coffee pot, not replace it. One question : would your coaches be willing to try a 5-stick kit for two weeks and give me an honest yes/no? One ask : if yes, I'll ship coach-only under a unique trial code. No invoice, no member-facing pitch. If the coaches say no, that ends it. — [Rep] Restless · [phone] 98 words · 1 screen · 1 Q · 1 ask · 0 attachments. Overshoot any — cut before send. B The Rules Enforced At The Comma Subject is a question. Never the product name. First line is their world. Not ours. One question. One ask. Never two. No hero claims. See banned list below. No attachments unless they ask. Label PDF lives in reply #2. Signature ≤ 2 lines. Name, brand, phone. If it requires scrolling on a phone — cut. Banned Language · Never In Email #1 "industry-leading" "most trusted" "proven" / "clinically proven" "best-in-class" "game-changing" "revolutionary" "the #1 choice" any unsourced % Use Instead Verbs & specifics. "Built for the back half." · "120 mg caffeine per stick." · "Coach-only trial, coded." C Fact Bank — What We Actually Know Honest Numbers · Cite Internally, Never In Email #1 · "I Don't Know — I'll Come Back By [Day]" Beats Guessing # Claim What We Can Say · Where It Comes From F1 Formulation 120 mg caffeine · 240 mg L-theanine · Rhodiola rosea · prebiotic fiber · magnesium bisglycinate · Na / K / Mg electrolytes. ~27 ingredients total. Confirm current mg against the Supplement Facts panel before written quotes. COA on request. F2 Sugar / calories 0 g added sugar. Verified on retail Nutrition panel. F3 Certifications Confirm with Ops before citing. Do not quote NSF / Informed Sport in email #1. # Claim What We Can Say · Where It Comes From F4 Trial mechanic Unique Shopify code per ship. Re-orders auto-tracked in CRM. F5 Price posture Ask Ops per lane. Never quote wholesale in email #1 — only after a trial-yes. F6 Case studies Named peer stories are okay ("Capt. [X] at [station]"). No aggregated %s without Ops sign-off. Rule / T09 — One screen. One question. One coded ask. Every number from the bank, not your head. 01 Screen · 02 Question · 03 Coded Ask · 04 Sourced

manualPages.label: Appendix · Rep Rules
manualPages.title: The Ten Rep Rules.
manualPages.text: Appendix · Rep Rules The Ten Rep Rules. ENTR-454 / APX Appendix House Rules 13 / 30 Purpose. The house rules of selling Restless. If any of these feel negotiable on a hard day, re-read. Every one of them exists because someone already broke it and cost us a lane. A Rules 01–05 · What You Ship Mechanics Of Every Trial 01 · Code before product. Every ship gets a unique Shopify code generated before the box leaves. No code — no ship. No exceptions. 02 · Trials, not coded trial packs. We ship 3–10 coded sticks, not full cases, and never "just to say thanks." A trial is a test with a yes/no at the end. 03 · Named recipient only. No trial ships to "the gym" or "the station." It ships to a named human who agreed to give honest feedback. 04 · Coach-only / crew-only first. Member-facing and public-facing pitches only happen after the coach, captain, or fitness lead says yes. 05 · Honest yes/no is the deliverable. If the crew says "chuck it," that's a clean result. Flag it, log it, don't push again for 90 days. B Rules 06–10 · What You Say Language Discipline 06 · No hero claims. Never "purpose-built," "proven," "well-regarded," or any number you can't point to in the fact bank. 07 · Cite the label, not the pitch deck. When a buyer wants a number, read it off the Supplement Facts panel. That's the source of truth. 08 · "I don't know" is a winning answer. Followed by a deadline: "— let me get you the exact figure by Thursday." 09 · The founder's story is the backdrop, not the pitch. Bring it out once, briefly, when a buyer asks why you. Then return to their world. 10 · Walk away clean. A real "no" is a gift. Thank them, log it, re-queue to 90 days. Pressure kills the next conversation too. C Pre-Send / Pre-Ship Self-Check Thirty Seconds Before The Email Leaves Or The Box Ships Question If The Answer Is "Not Sure" — Do This Did I use a number I can't point to in the fact bank? Strip it. Replace with "we can share the full label on request." Is there a unique trial code on this ship? Don't ship. Generate it in Shopify first. Is the recipient a named human who asked for a trial? Don't ship. Ask first. Would a respected peer cringe at the adjectives in this email? Cut them. The verbs are doing the work. If this buyer says "no" in 24 hours, do I know what I'll do? Thank them, log in CRM, re-queue to 90-day. No second ask today. Does this email or talk track cite the founder's story more than twice? Dial it back. Use it once, in their context, then move on. Close / Part One — Ship honest, ship coded, walk away clean. 01 Honest · 02 Coded · 03 Named · 04 Clean

manualPages.label: Start Here
manualPages.title: Field Entry
manualPages.text: Part Two · Field Operating System The OS That Keeps The Book Honest Start Here Field Entry Section 14 / 30 1 of 2 V0 Live Flow Field evidence flows left through the four gears, then drops into the 8-field Activation Standard below. Move only when each gear shows an observable signal. 1 Hear Named Pain Can the buyer say the problem in their own words? ↓ 2 Confirm Access Can we reach the buyer, influencer, or channel? ↓ 3 Shape Trial Fit What is the smallest credible test? ↓ 4 Run Feedback Loop What response will teach us what to do next? ↓ 5 Fill Activation Standard Do the 8 fields now have evidence? (see below) ↓ 6 Decide: Advance · Revise · Park Move only when the signal is strong enough. Golden Rule: No named pain + no access + no trial fit + no feedback loop = honest out. Walk away clean — a real "no" is a gift. Do not ghost. Code it and move. The Activation Standard (8 Field) Each gear above must produce evidence in the 8 fields before a sample ships. Segment is metadata — not a gate. # Field Example (Good) 1 Named Human "Sgt. Ramirez, MWR coordinator" 2 Current Routine "Crew buys energy drinks out of pocket" 3 Real Use Window "Pre-PT, 0530, 3x/week" 4 Buyer-Named Risk "Sgt. Ramirez worried crew will mock it" 5 Trial / Review Type 1 box (20 sticks) · 12 crew × 1–2 shifts 6 Unique Code "MRZ-0526-A" — every trial gets a code 7 Follow-Up Date + Channel "Check Monday after weekend ruck. Text me." 8 Binary Success Q + Route "If 8+ say yes, I'll bring the PO. If not, honest out." Segment Quick-Pick Segment Entry Door Champion Title Social Risk First Responder Station house, training academy Captain, Training Officer, Union Steward High — peer mockery if product fails Military / MWR MWR office, unit readiness brief MWR Coordinator, CSM, Platoon Sergeant Moderate — chain of command visibility Healthcare Nursing admin, shift supervisor Charge Nurse, Dept Manager, CNO High — clinical scrutiny culture Industrial Site safety meeting, break room Site Supervisor, Safety Officer, Union Rep Low-Mod — peer adoption drives decision Gym / Combat Head coach, owner, fight camp Head Coach, Gym Owner, Athletic Director Moderate — athlete trust is currency First-Meeting Goal by Segment Responder: Get the captain to try it on shift Military: MWR sample → unit trial Healthcare: Evidence pack → procurement review (no trial until they ask) Industrial: Safety officer endorsement → crew sample Gym: Coach tries pre-workout → athlete sees it Field Tip: Never leave a first meeting without a named human, a use window, and a feedback date. If you don't have all three, you have a conversation — not an activation. START HERE — Field Entry & Activation Standard 14 / 30

manualPages.label: Start Here
manualPages.title: Trial Discipline
manualPages.text: Start Here Trial Discipline Section 15 / 30 2 of 2 Segment Proof-Pack Inventory One observable signal per cell — no assumed pain, no theoretical access, no trial without a feedback loop. Segment Named Pain Access Proof Trial Fit Signal Feedback Loop First Responder "3 PM crash on a 24-hour shift" — said by the captain Named captain or training officer at one station 1 box · squad of 12 · 2 shifts · coded Captain text Monday after weekend ruck: 8+ thumbs-up? Military / MWR "Hour 10 ruck wall — energy drinks not allowed" MWR coordinator or platoon sergeant agrees to host 3 boxes · unit of 30–60 · 1 week · coded MWR coord debrief after FTX: did the back half hold? Healthcare "Afternoon slump · review standards named by charge nurse" Charge nurse + procurement clears label review first Conditional trial only after evidence + label review pass Shift supervisor signs review form · feedback returned Industrial "Heat + fatigue at hour 7" — named by site safety officer Site safety officer endorses; supervisor opens break room 2 boxes · crew of 20–40 · 1 week · coded Safety officer logs uptake count + crew feedback Gym / Combat "Endurance wall at hour 2–3" — named by head coach Head coach uses visibly; athletes see it in the gym 2 boxes · 15–30 athletes · 1 week · coded Coach reports enough value to consider more · re-order ask Discipline: Do not advance a segment until each proof-pack cell has at least one observable signal. No assumed pain. No theoretical access. No trial without a feedback loop. Trial Size Guide Segment Crew Size Sample Size Duration First Responder 12–20 1 box (20 sticks) 2 shifts Military / MWR 30–60 3 boxes 1 week Healthcare 8–15 1 box 3 shifts Industrial 20–40 2 boxes 1 week Gym / Combat 15–30 2 boxes 1 week Too Big = Fail: Oversized trials overwhelm champions. Start smaller than you think. A 5-person trial that succeeds beats a 50-person trial that fizzles. Social Risk Mitigation Taste Risk: Champion tries it alone first Policy Risk: Check label with procurement before trial Crew Mockery: Alpha / coach tries it visibly first Chain-of-Command: Get informal nod before formal ask Four Gears · Qualification Gear Ask Pass Signal → Activation Field 1 · Named Pain "What does the crew run into at hour 3 — in their own words?" Buyer names the pain unprompted → Fields 2, 3, 4 2 · Access "Who can actually run a trial — and who signs off?" Named human + path to sign-off → Field 1 3 · Trial Fit "What is the smallest credible test for your crew?" Concrete size, window, and code agreed → Fields 5, 6 4 · Feedback Loop "What response tells us continue, adjust, or stop?" Date, channel, binary success Q set → Fields 7, 8 Rule: All four gears must produce evidence before a trial ships. If any gear is empty, you are pitching — not qualifying. A real "no" closes the gear cleanly; do not advance with a missing signal. Field Kit Checklist (Every Rep, Every Day) ☐ Product samples (3 boxes min) ☐ Label + ingredient transparency card ☐ Founder story (30 sec) ☐ 5-slide deck (phone + print) ☐ Whiteboard marker + napkin ☐ Trial tracking sheet ☐ Business cards + QR code START HERE — Trial Discipline & Field Kit 15 / 30

manualPages.label: Tool 10 L2 — Diagnose This
manualPages.title: Story Vault
manualPages.text: Tool 10 L2 — Diagnose This Story Vault Tool 16 / 30 Story Vault Use when Buyer asks "why this?", rep needs credibility, or a champion fears social risk. Run this Pick one story card, tell it in 15/45 seconds, then ask for one clean next step. Capture New buyer phrase, real use window, and what risk the story reduced. Four Moves 1 Curate — Capture real moments ↓ 2 Categorize — Match to theme ↓ 3 Construct — Five C's arc ↓ 4 Convey — 15s / 45s / 90s 5 Narrative Themes 1. The Back Half "Restless is built for the working window the current routine fades through — not for a quick taste test." 2. Trust the Label "You should not trust a new brand because of a story. Start with the label, then let one real use window decide." 3. Crew Choice "I'm not asking you to sell it to the crew. I'm asking permission for the crew to judge it." 4. One Stick "Most people aren't buying one product. They're building a routine out of two or three. Restless tests whether one stick can simplify that." 5. Made for My Shift "This is not a wellness initiative. It is a small test for people already buying energy products to get through demanding work." Worked Example: Station Table How Story Card B maps across the Five C's. Use this as your template. C1 Character Captain who did not want to push anything new on the crew C2 Context Crew already had coffee and cans in the station routine C3 Conflict Captain feared crew would mock or ignore a new product C4 Climax Rep reframed: "Do not sell it. Put a small coded pack on the table and let the crew decide." C5 Closure Trial protected the captain and gave the product a fair real-shift test 15-second: "I'm not asking you to start a program. I'm asking for a small station-table test and one honest yes/no." 45-second: "The captain didn't want to push anything new. The crew already had their routine. The risk was mockery. The turn was a table test — not a sell. The win was a fair real-shift verdict that protected the captain's credibility." Story Matrix™ · 6-Card Library Each card is one Type × Use Case coordinate in Wortmann's Story Matrix. Optional expanded examples in Appendix A (p.17) . Card Type (column) Use Case (row) A Failure No-ship discipline · onboarding B Success First-responder trial ask · crew autonomy C Success Gym / combat sports discovery D Legend Healthcare · MWR · policy-gated buyers E Legend Buyer asks "Why you?" F Template Extract your first rep-earned story Rep Capture Buyer words: Next action: Follow-up date: Tool 10 — Story Vault & Narrative Themes 16 / 30

manualPages.label: Appendix A · Optional Story Library
manualPages.title: Optional Story Library
manualPages.text: Appendix A · Optional Story Library Optional Story Library Appendix 17 / 30 Cards A–F The six cards referenced in Tool 10 — expanded. Color band = type: Failure · Success · Legend · Template . Each Five C's line is compressed to its working form; the signature line is what you say live. A · The Dead Sample FAILURE Use: No-ship discipline, new-rep onboarding, founder coaching. C1. A new rep with good intentions and a station that sounded interested. C2. "Sure, send some over." No named owner, no use window, no follow-up date. C3. Product left the building. Budget spent. No one knew if it was used, ignored, lost, or disliked. C4. At follow-up, nobody could answer. A shipment, not a signal. C5. No code, no owner, no use window, no follow-up means no ship. "Sending product is not progress. Without a named owner, use window, code, and follow-up, it's inventory leaving the building. Every Restless trial ends in yes / no." B · Station Table SUCCESS Use: First-responder trial ask, coffee/Monster routine, crew autonomy. C1. A captain who did not want to push anything new on the crew. C2. The crew already had coffee and cans in the station routine. C3. Not the formula first — whether the crew would mock or ignore a new product. C4. Rep reframed: "Don't sell it. Put a small coded pack on the table. Let the crew decide." C5. Station trial protected the captain and gave the product a fair real-shift test. "I'm not asking you to start a program. I'm asking for a small station-table test and one honest yes / no." C · The Coach's Caveat SUCCESS Use: Gym / combat sports discovery. C1. A coach constantly asked: "What should I take before training?" C2. The gym already has Celsius, C4, Ghost, coffee, and pre-workout in the building. C3. Every recommendation comes with a caveat: caffeine, no electrolytes, hidden blends, wrong for long sessions. C4. Rep asks for a coach-only trial before any shelf or member conversation. C5. The coach's credibility is protected — they test personally before recommending. "I'd rather you try it yourself before I ask for shelf space. If you wouldn't use it before training, your members shouldn't hear about it." D · Label Before Sample LEGEND Use: Healthcare, MWR, policy-gated buyers. C1. A healthcare or military buyer who has seen too many supplement pitches. C2. They ask for proof, policy status, or the label before discussing trial. C3. A normal rep would push samples. A Restless rep slows down. C4. Rep sends Supplement Facts, dose rationale, claim guardrails, and current certification status — first. C5. In review-heavy channels, restraint is credibility. Evidence earns the right to trial. "For your lane, I'd rather be reviewed than sampled. If the label doesn't pass, the product shouldn't move." E · Founder Story LEGEND Use: Buyer asks "Why you?" or "Where did this come from?" C1. Daniel, the founder — a Marine veteran building for people with long, demanding days. C2. The buyer wants to know whether Restless is authentic or just tactical packaging. C3. Founder background builds trust — but sounds exploitative if a rep overuses it. C4. Rep uses Daniel's story once, then returns to the buyer's routine, the label, and the trial. C5. Founder credibility opens the door. Label and use earn the room. "Daniel's background explains why the product exists. Your label review and one real use window decide whether it belongs here." F · Your First Story TEMPLATE Use: Fill after your first real field interaction. Start with the raw scene, then shape it. 1. Replay the scene. Where · Who · Exact phrase · What they were doing · What made it hard · What you did · What changed · The lesson. 2. Convert to Five C's. Map scene → Character / Context / Conflict / Climax / Closure. 3. Build the field version. Write the 15-second line, then the 45-second version. 4. Manager field-test. Real interaction? · Protects privacy? · Avoids unsupported claims? · Makes the buyer the hero? · Ends in a useful principle? · Under 45 seconds? Rule: one rep-earned story per week, from a real interaction. Stories from imagination do not count. Appendix A — Optional Story Library (Cards A–F) 17 / 30

manualPages.label: Tool 11 L2 — Diagnose This
manualPages.title: Ask & Listen
manualPages.text: Tool 11 L2 — Diagnose This Ask & Listen Tool 18 / 30 Ask & Listen Use when Discovery starts, buyer gives vague pushback, or “we already have coffee” appears. Run this Ask → mirror exact words → clarify meaning → qualify authority → ask one impact question. Capture Buyer phrase, emotional signal, institutional blocker, next question. Five Question Types 1 Connection — Build rapport 2 Discovery — Uncover routine 3 Qualifying — Test authority 4 Clarifying — Confirm meaning 5 Impact — Surface stakes Listening Ladder Level Hear Do L1 Words Respond literally L2 Feeling Name the emotion L3 Stakes Connect to identity First Question by Segment Segment Opener First Responder "Captain, what is the crew actually reaching for on overnights these days?" Military / MWR "What does the current fitness or readiness routine look like for your unit?" Healthcare "How do your people stay alert through long or rotating shifts?" Industrial "What's the longest hot shift, and what do crews currently use?" Gym / Combat "What do your athletes grab before training — and how do they feel at hour two?" Listen-for-This: "We already have coffee" = fact, habit defense, or clean no. Mirror before moving. Segment Pain Map Need Responder Military Health Indust Gym Functional Shift alertness Readiness Long hours Hot shifts Endurance Social Crew choice Unit cohesion Team norms Peer respect Coach trust Emotional Duty + fear Pride + discipline Compassion fatigue Family income Identity Institutional Policy / union Regs / MWR Procurement Safety / EHS Owner ROI Gold cell = primary leverage to lead with for that segment. Live Question Bank — one useful question by type and segment Type Responder Military Healthcare Industrial Gym Connection "Long tour today?" "Training week or normal duty day?" "Day shift or nights?" "Hot floor this week?" "Busy class block?" Discovery "What do they grab on overnights?" "What is the current readiness routine?" "What do staff use at 3 AM?" "What sits in the break room?" "What do athletes use before class?" Qualifying "Who owns a station trial?" "Who signs off before a test?" "Who reviews the label?" "Who can place a box?" "Who decides what coaches test?" Clarifying "When you say coffee works, when does it stop?" "When you say approved, approved by whom?" "When you say evidence, what format?" "When you say too expensive, vs. what?" "When you say members won't care, who would?" Impact "What would make the crew embrace it vs. roast it?" "What would protect your credibility?" "What would make review easy?" "What makes a break-room product survive?" "What would make you use it before training?" Rep Capture Buyer words: Next action: Follow-up date: Tool 11 — Ask & Listen: Segment Pain Map 18 / 30

manualPages.label: Tool 12 L2 — Diagnose This
manualPages.title: Skepticism & Objections
manualPages.text: Tool 12 L2 — Diagnose This Skepticism & Objections Tool 19 / 30 Objections Use when Buyer resists, asks for proof, pushes price, or a trial is not clean. Run this Encourage → clarify → confirm → respond → check. If no clean path, Honest Out. Capture Objection code, safe route, whether to trial / evidence / retime / close. 5-Step Reframe K=Knowledge · S=Skill · D=Discipline 1 Encourage D — "That's a fair point." ↓ 2 Clarify S — "Help me understand…" ↓ 3 Confirm D — "So what I'm hearing is…" ↓ 4 Respond K — Bridge to Restless frame ↓ 5 Check D — "Does that address it?" Honest Out BATNA — If no named owner, no feedback loop, oversized ask, or social risk too high: "I want to be straight with you — this doesn't meet our trial standard right now. Can I check back when [condition]?" Hard Claim Guardrails Do NOT Say Say Instead "Proven to reduce errors" "Formulated for energy + hydration; trial decides fit" "Clinically tested" "Ingredients with published research" "Safe for everyone" "Review the label with your doctor" "Better than [competitor]" "Different formula — here's the label" "Guaranteed energy" "Designed for a steadier use-window test" Master Objection Matrix Match buyer line to row — apply 5-Step Reframe (left). Decode tells you what's really being said. Safe Route is what you ask; Bridge is what you say. # Buyer Says Decode (What They Mean) Safe Route (Ask) Bridge (Say) 1 "We already have coffee." Habit defense · functional claim · clean no "When does coffee work well, and when does it stop being enough?" "That's the window I'd test, not argue about." 2 "The crew won't care." Social risk · no champion · prior failure "Has a coach or alpha tried it visibly?" "We protect the champion's credibility. Start with the alpha." 3 "Send me some info." Stall · not interested · needs social proof "What info would change a trial to yes?" "I'll bring the label, a sample, and a sample Activation Standard. 10 minutes." 4 "Is it OPSS-approved?" Military / procurement check · risk aversion "What does approval mean for your process?" "I won't claim certification I can't verify. Here's the label and current status." 5 "It's too expensive." Price vs. value · budget · comparison "Cost per serving vs. what the crew buys now?" "One stick replaces the coffee + hydration + pre-workout stack." 6 "120mg isn't enough." High tolerance · comparison to 300mg products "What does the crew need at hour 6 — a spike or a window?" "Designed for a steadier use-window test, not a spike. Let one real use window decide." 7 "We use Celsius / Monster." Brand loyalty · habit · social norm "What does the crew do at hour 3?" "Different format: one stick in water, with the label visible." 8 "Never heard of you." Trust gap · credibility · risk aversion Founder story (Card E) + evidence pack + retime if needed "Fair. We were built for this market, not the grocery aisle. Here's why." Honest Out Mechanics — When the 5-Step Doesn't Apply Core Script: "I want to be straight with you — this doesn't meet our trial standard right now. Can I check back when [specific condition]?" Trigger Code Re-engage When No Named Owner — "I'll ask around" NO-OWNER They name a human. No Feedback Loop — "Let me know how it goes" NO-LOOP They commit to a check-in date. Oversized Ask — 50-person trial, zero prior use OVERSIZE Counter: "Protect your credibility. Start with 5." High Social Risk — champion untested, crew skeptical HIGH-RISK Counter: "Start with the alpha. Visible win first." Clean Activation Threshold Named Owner Feedback Loop Trial Discipline CLEAN TO TRIAL = All three intersect. No "no" yet — but no activation without all three. Walk away clean if any are missing. Rep Capture Buyer words: Next action: Follow-up date: Tool 12 — Skepticism & Objections Matrix 19 / 30

manualPages.label: Tool 13 L0 — Run This
manualPages.title: Visual Impact
manualPages.text: Tool 13 L0 — Run This Visual Impact Tool 20 / 30 Visual Impact Use when Buyer needs a simple picture before a trial ask. Run this Draw the working-window sketch as illustrative design intent; the trial decides fit. Capture Current routine fade point, proposed use window, dated yes/no follow-up. U.S. Harbor Authority · District 7 Form HA-12 · Tide Table · Posted Notice Lat 41°N · Long 71°W The Working Window Current routine fades → test one real use window Rep line: “Where does the current routine fade, and what do they reach for next?” 00 03 06 09 12 0 3 6 9 12 HOUR OF PASSAGE TIDE HEIGHT · FT ABOVE CHART DATUM ABOVE TARGET CHANNEL NAVIGABLE WINDOW BELOW WORKING WINDOW SOLID · RESTLESS DESIGN INTENT DASHED · SPIKE-AND-FADE ROUTINE HARBOR TIDE TABLE · 12-HOUR PASSAGE · EXAMPLE STATION Illustrative · station-issue draft · soundings in feet above chart datum · winds & weather not modeled Reading the Chart 1. Three water-level zones — above target channel (overload), navigable window (functional output), below navigable draft (the fade-out zone). 2. The dashed line represents a familiar spike-and-fade routine that may leave the working window before the use case is over. 3. The solid line represents Restless's design intent: a lower-dose, theanine-paired energy-plus-hydration option meant to be tested in a real use window. 4. Ask the buyer: "When in the passage does your current routine fade - and what are they reaching for next?" 5. Hand them the chart, then ask for one real use window and one dated yes/no follow-up. Chart Legend Solid — Restless design intent (test in one real window) Dashed — Familiar spike-and-fade routine Channel band — Navigable window (the functional test zone) Segment Tide Tables Segment / Port Passage Length Tide-Out Window First Responder 24-hour passage Hour 14–18 · overnight ebb Military / MWR Field-exercise day Hour 10–12 · ruck-march drawdown Healthcare 12-hour shift Hour 6–8 · afternoon ebb Industrial 10-hour hot shift Hour 7–9 · heat + fatigue Gym / Combat Training session Hour 2–3 · endurance wall Rep Capture Buyer words: Next action: Follow-up date: Tool 13 — Visual Impact: Harbor Tide Table · The Navigable-Window Chart 20 / 30

manualPages.label: Tool 14 L1 — Fill This Out
manualPages.title: Progress Report
manualPages.text: Tool 14 L1 — Fill This Out Progress Report Tool 21 / 30 Progress Report Use when Monthly external update, supporter/investor communication, or audience-facing progress. Run this Job → audience → cadence → categories → design. One page, one ask, one offer. Capture Headline phrase, movement metric, learning, proof, ask, offer. Package yourself and showcase your brand so it is meaningful and memorable to your audience. Contents depend on your personal brand, your audience, and the job you need the report to do. Send one useful page, not a diary. 5-Step Build Step Decision Restless rep example 1 · Job What job should this report do — and for whom? Build trust with supporters; recruit useful help. 2 · Audience Who reads it. Who can forward it. Investors, friends, partners, advisors, supporters. 3 · Cadence Same day each cycle. Skip a month, lose the brand. Monthly · first Monday · same format. 4 · Categories Pick the 4–6 that match the audience and the job. Movement · Learning · Proof · Ask · Offer. 5 · Design Look and feel reflect your brand. Visual impact matters. One page · brick header · buyer phrase as headline. Restless Monthly Template Field Write this Limit Subject What changed + why it matters. 10 words Headline The one buyer phrase or motion that defines the month. 1 line Movement Trials, activations, reorders, referrals, clean no's. 3 bullets Learning Buyer language or objection that changed the next move. 2 bullets Proof One quote · one number · one named place. 3 lines One Ask Specific intro, review, or route needed now. 1 line One Offer Something the reader can forward without explaining. 1 line Jobs to Be Done — Pick One Three Wortmann patterns. Restless reps default to Sales Progress · Monthly . Founders may also run Networking · Quarterly for syndicate supporters. Job Audience Cadence Categories Job Search 50 employer targets Weekly About me · Goals · K/S/D · Quotes · Lessons · Offer · Ask Sales Progress Sales team / manager Monthly Progress vs goals · Coaching needed · Feedback received · Network adds · Lessons · Offer · Customer success Networking My network / supporters Quarterly Current story · What you're reading · Challenges · Help needed · What's next · Offer · Ask Filled Example — May Update Mock example only — names, numbers, and quotes below are illustrative. Replace with verified numbers and approved quotes before sending externally. Subject: May — station trials live, coach reorders, one intro ask. Headline: "A smoother working window." — Capt. Torres (illustrative). Movement: 31 qualified convos · 16 coded activations · 4 reorders · 3 referrals · 5 clean no's. Learning: "We don't need another wellness thing" answered with label transparency + small trial under their rules. One Ask: Warm intro to a Phoenix fire captain considering a station-bag trial in June. One Offer: Redacted Activation Standard template — for any early CPG founder. Design Rule One scrollable page. Brand voice in the headline. Visual impact ≠ chartjunk. Same look every month so readers learn the shape and can scan in 30 seconds. Do not include: vanity metrics, unverified safety or clinical claims, internal politics, diary paragraphs, more than one ask, or more than one forward. Internal CRM scoreboard, 1:1 agenda, and rep-development rubric live in Appendix B · Manager Coaching Companion — not in the audience-facing report. manualPages.label: Tool 15 L1 — Fill This Out
manualPages.title: Weekly Cadence
manualPages.text: Tool 15 L1 — Fill This Out Weekly Cadence Tool 22 / 30 Weekly Cadence Use when Sunday planning, Friday review, or the week is becoming reactive. Run this Contact blocks first; development daily; admin smallest; one expert move per week. Capture Segment lane, protected call blocks, expert move, one thing to say no to. Priorities first. All else can wait. Use your calendar as a doing device — block selling time before inbox, meetings, or admin. 45-hour week (8am–5pm, Mon–Fri) maps to the breakdown below. Order of Priority — Weekly Time Mix # Priority Activities % Hours 1 Contact & Forward Progress Prospecting calls · Client calls & meetings · Networking 40% 18h 2 Development Practice · Feedback · Reflection 20% 9h 3 Communication Follow-up · Proposals · Above & Beyond · Story Capture 18% 8h 4 Internal Internal meetings · Updating presentations · Check-ins 12% 5.5h 5 Tracking CRM updates · Revenue projections · Pipeline review 7% 3h 6 Administrative Scheduling · Organizing · Company meetings · Reporting 3% 1.5h Rule: Contact blocks go on the calendar first. Development happens daily. Admin gets the smallest named container — never the morning. If admin expands, move it to Friday PM or cut the lane size. What to Say "No" To Trap Why it kills the week Inbox before the contact block Reactive mode replaces priority order. "Quick" internal meeting in AM Steals the highest-leverage selling hour. Open-ended sample request No named owner, no feedback date — ghost. Mixed-segment call block Code-switching tax erases the muscle. Calendar as a Doing Device Sunday Setup · 15 minutes before bed → Schedule priorities first → Block times for selling first → What other 3 things HAVE to get done? → What Knowledge / Skill / Discipline will I develop? → What will I say "no" to? Friday Check-In · 30 minutes before leaving → Check in with buddy on progress against goals → Any outstanding to-do's? → Re-confirm next week's meetings Ideal Week Snapshot Mon Tue Wed Thu Fri AM calls PM list build AM calls PM follow-up AM field visits PM CRM AM calls PM proposals AM close loops PM report Expert Move of the Week Pick one per week. Practice · feedback · reflection. Knowledge, Skill, and Discipline compound only if you choose. Story One credibility moment, retold under 60s. Objection Five-step reframe, drilled with a peer. Impact Question One question that opens the buyer's routine. Visual Stack / Curve / Loop drawn live in 30s. Follow-Up Binary question + dated route. Qualification Four-Gear pass in under 4 minutes. Pipeline tracking, lane selectors, and 1:1 manager prep live in Appendix B · Manager Coaching Companion (next page) — subordinate to the cadence above, not a substitute for it. Rep Capture Buyer words: Next action: Follow-up date: Tool 15 — Weekly Cadence: Priorities First. All Else Can Wait. 22 / 30

manualPages.label: Appendix · B Manager Coaching
manualPages.title: Manager Coaching Companion
manualPages.text: Appendix · B Manager Coaching Manager Coaching Companion Appendix 23 / 30 Manager Coaching Internal weekly material that complements Tools 14 and 15 — not a substitute. Tool 14 is the audience-facing monthly Progress Report; Tool 15 is the rep's selling-first cadence. This page captures the manager-coaching artifacts that live in the CRM and the 1:1 — kept here so the graded tools stay clean and audience-true. Internal Weekly Report (CRM) Typed into the CRM every Friday. 90 seconds or it belongs in the 1:1. # Field Prompt 1 Scoreboard Activations · convos · qualified · outcomes 2 Manager Ask What do you need to go faster? Friday Rule: CRM gets numbers. The 1:1 gets stories. No paragraphs in the CRM. Verbal 1:1 Agenda Five topics. One-line prep each. The manager listens for buyer language, not rep self-report. Topic One-line prep Best Phrase Exact buyer words worth reusing Best Movement What advanced this week? Cleanest No What did you learn from a no? One Learning What do you know now that you didn't? K / S / D Knowledge · Skill · Discipline gap Pre-Call Check Did the rep load the right quickbar tool for this segment? Sample Friday Entry 1. Scoreboard: 14 convos · 8 qualified · 3 trials live · 1 placement (Example Station — illustrative). 2. Manager Ask: Intro to former military rep who cracked MWR at Bragg. 1:1 Agenda: Best Phrase — "We don't need more energy. We need a smoother working window." (Capt. Torres — illustrative) · Cleanest No — IND-0526-C, 90-day pilot req · K/S/D — Knowledge gap on supplement regs. Rep Development Rubric Manager-scored. Rep self-report is input, not verdict. Promotion requires evidence in all three columns at the next level. Level Knowledge Skill Discipline 1 · New Label + basic claims Scripts + checklists Shows up, logs calls 2 · Field-Ready Segment culture + objections Adapts in real time Follows up, tracks codes 3 · Trusted Competitive landscape Coaches others Builds pipeline, forecasts 4 · Peer-Level Product + market + ops Creates plays, content Owns segment, mentors 8 Credibility Baselines Manager checks these during the verbal 1:1. The weakest baseline drives the rep's Expert Move of the Week in Tool 15. 1 · Product & Label Fluency 2 · Energy Drink Landscape 3 · Supplement Regulations 4 · Human Performance Basics 5 · Segment Culture 6 · Trial Mechanics 7 · Storytelling 8 · Visual Impact Do not inflate: rubric levels and baseline scores belong to the manager, not rep self-report. If everyone is Level 3, the rubric is broken — recalibrate against a named field example. Weekly Pipeline Tracker Status discipline for every named account. Code everything — ghosts are pipeline debt and walk-away clean beats walk-away vague. Status Meaning Action ✓ Live Trial running · awaiting review Calendar the review date ◐ Paused Paused for a named condition Name condition + reactivation date ✗ Clean No Clear reject · lesson captured Code and close · thank the buyer — Ghost No contact in 14 days One re-engage attempt, then archive Weekly Lane Selector Sunday's first decision (Tool 15 Sunday Setup). Pick one segment lane for the week. No mixing inside a call block. Field This Week Primary Segment ________________________ Why Now ________________________ Top 10 Targets 1. ___ 2. ___ 3. ___ 4. ___ 5. ___ 6. ___ 7. ___ 8. ___ 9. ___ 10. ___ What Not to Do ________________________ Mix Rule One segment per call block. "A real no is a gift." Clean no beats ghosted yes. Code everything — protect the rep, the buyer, and the next conversation. manualPages.label: Appendix C · Visual Frameworks
manualPages.title: Visual Frameworks
manualPages.text: Appendix C · Visual Frameworks Visual Frameworks Appendix C 24 / 30 Visual Frameworks 4 Hip-Pocket Frameworks — sketches the rep redraws on a napkin, whiteboard, or station table 1 One Stick vs. Stack → stack one stick Draw: 3 stacked boxes left, 1 wide box right, arrow between. Say: "Most people aren't buying one product — they're building a routine. The test is whether one stick covers enough of it." 2 Three Gates USE WINDOW → SOCIAL RISK → REVIEW BAR Draw: 3 boxes in a row, arrows between. Bottom rule in red. Say: "Three gates before anything ships: where, who, and what standard. If one's missing we retime — not push." 3 Activation Loop NAMED OWNER → USE WINDOW → CODED TRIAL → BINARY Q → ROUTE Draw: 5 boxes left-to-right, dashed loop arrow on top. Say: "Every activation has five parts. Skip any one and we've shipped without a signal — that's the part we won't do." 4 Champion Protection TASTE · POLICY · CREW MOCKERY → CHAMPION Draw: Circle for the champion, dashed ring around them, three risk arrows inbound. Say: "Our job is to keep three risks off the champion's plate. If the trial can embarrass them, it's the wrong trial." Segment Visual Quick-Map Segment Best Visual Object to Use Words to Write First Responder Working-Window Chart Whiteboard, napkin "Tide-out at hour 14" vs. "test the working window" Military / MWR One Stick vs. Stack Water bottle, sticks "One stick. One bottle. Done." Healthcare Three Gates Clipboard, label "Use window, social risk, review bar" Industrial Spike vs. Smoother Whiteboard, marker "Spike at break vs. smooth through shift" Gym / Combat Activation Loop Whiteboard, phone "Named human → coded trial → binary check" 5-Slide Deck Rules 1. Image first, text second 2. One claim per slide 3. No competitor names 4. Label is the source of truth 5. End with a small next step Guardrail: Do not draw guaranteed duration, clinical outcomes, or competitor danger. Draw design intent and test logic only. Appendix C — Visual Frameworks Reference 24 / 30

manualPages.label: Appendix D · Activation Threshold
manualPages.title: Clean Activation Threshold
manualPages.text: Appendix D · Activation Threshold Clean Activation Threshold Appendix D 25 / 30 Activation Threshold 3 Named Owner One named human who will own trial, review, and next step. Not "the team," not "I think the sergeant." A real name with a real title. Test: Can you text them? 3 Feedback Loop Binary question + specific date + method. Example: "Would the crew use it again in the same window? Check Monday after shift. Text me." Test: Would a stranger know what to check? 3 Trial Discipline Small enough to protect credibility. Visible enough to get noticed. Long enough to produce feedback. If any leg breaks, the stool falls. Test: Would the champion's reputation survive a fail? Clean No Checklist ✗ No named owner ("I'll ask around") ✗ No feedback loop ("Let me know how it goes") ✗ Oversized ask (50-person trial, no prior use) ✗ Social risk too high (champion untested, crew skeptical) ✗ No decision maker identified ("The committee decides") ✗ No use window ("Whenever they want") Honest Out Script: "I want to be straight with you — this doesn't meet our trial standard right now. I don't want to waste your time or mine. Can I check back when [specific condition]?" Activation Frequency Activity Frequency Goal Calls (outbound) Daily 10–15 dials Emails 3× / week Follow-up, samples, check-ins Touchpoints 2× / week Champion nurture, trial check Account planning 1× / week Pipeline review, route optimization Field visits 2–3× / week Face-to-face = highest close rate Peer review 1× / week Role-play objection handling 4-Week Activation Cycle Week 1: Qualify + named owner + use window Week 2: Sample delivered, trial begins, feedback Q set Week 3: Feedback loop — gather response, surface objections Week 4: Binary close — placement, expansion, or honest out Appendix D — Clean Activation Threshold 25 / 30

manualPages.label: Appendix E · Operating Cards
manualPages.title: 6 Operating Cards
manualPages.text: Appendix E · Operating Cards 6 Operating Cards Appendix E 26 / 30 Operating Cards 1 Founder Story One-sentence: Marine veteran built Restless for the people he watched run on Pop-Tarts and Monster. Use when: Buyer says “Never heard of you” or “Where did this come from?” 30-sec story (rehearse verbatim): “Daniel, the founder, was a Marine — last duty station was the Marine Corps Recruit Depot, where he watched people work 140-hour weeks on Pop-Tarts and Monster. His dad in construction did the same thing with Code Red. His brother is a police officer. Same pattern, same crash. Restless is what he wishes those guys had had — one stick, visible-label ingredients, designed for the back half of a long day. Can I send a small coded trial so your crew can test it on one real shift?” Rule: Story is backdrop, not the pitch. End every telling by returning to their shift, crew, or trial window. Source: Founder transcript (APX G). 2 Label Defense One-sentence: What every ingredient does and why it's there. Use when: Buyer asks "What's in it?" or "Is it safe?" Talk track: “120 mg caffeine — about a strong coffee. 240 mg L-theanine paired with it to control the stress response. Rhodiola rosea, prebiotic fiber, magnesium bisglycinate, electrolytes. Full label, no proprietary blends — read the current Supplement Facts panel for exact mg per ingredient.” 3 Competitive Edge One-sentence: Market context without naming competitors. Use when: Buyer says "We use Celsius / Monster / coffee." Talk track: "Most energy formats are built for a fast spike. Restless is designed for a steadier use-window test. Different format: one stick in water, with the label visible." 4 Regulatory Proof One-sentence: The label is the source of truth — nothing more, nothing less. Use when: Buyer asks "Is it approved?" Talk track: “Current certification status is listed in Appendix G and should be confirmed for the SKU before I put it in writing. Restless has been Informed Sport Certified per founder; the program is third-party verified and recognized by the DOD, and the formula carries no DOD banned substances. I won't say “OPSS-approved” or “OPSS-certified” because OPSS is a program, not a certification mark. If certification is your gate, send me the standard and I'll come back with the current document.” Healthcare caution: Do not lead healthcare buyers with “Stay in the Fight”; lead with label, evidence, and review process. 5 Trial Mechanics — How to Prove Fit Without Overclaiming Use when: Buyer asks "Has anyone else tried this?" and you do not have an attributable, written case study to cite. Who the trial is for: A named champion (captain, MWR coordinator, charge nurse, head coach) with a crew of 12–30 and one real shift to test on. What it tests: A specific shift or moment — the hour-3 fade on a 24-hour, the ruck drawdown, the 2 p.m. floor slump. What the rep watches for: Did the champion finish a stick? Did the crew ask where to get more? Did the champion text back unprompted? Counts as a reorder signal: "Send more" within 7 days, or 8+ thumbs-up from a 12-person crew. Counts as a clean no: Champion silent at the agreed follow-up date, or "It's fine, but we'll stick with what we have." Code it. Move. 6 Science Brief REP-ONLY — DO NOT QUOTE TO HEALTHCARE BUYERS Audience: Internal coaching only. Useful for rep credibility on analytical objections; not a buyer-facing talk track and not a clinical claim. Use when: Rep is preparing for a science-literate buyer and wants ingredient-level orientation, NOT to recite to a healthcare buyer. Coaching frame: Caffeine + 240 mg L-theanine has published ingredient-level research on attention and stress modulation. Rhodiola rosea is studied for acute-stress adaptation. Prebiotic fiber is included as part of the formula architecture; do not describe absorption effects unless the evidence pack supports it. Magnesium bisglycinate is included as a bioavailable magnesium form; do not claim jitter reduction. Electrolytes (Na / K / Mg) are part of the hydration formula. None of this is a clinical outcome claim — the label is still the source of truth, and none of this language is for buyer-facing use. Reference — Full Objection Matrix Live reference: Tool 12 (page 19) — the full 8-objection matrix is inline, no flipping required. Next page (Appendix F) is the printable wall-card version: larger type, evidence-pack stack. Appendix E — 6 Operating Cards 26 / 30

manualPages.label: Appendix F · Objection Matrix
manualPages.title: Objection Matrix
manualPages.text: Appendix F · Objection Matrix Objection Matrix Appendix F 27 / 30 Objection Matrix # Buyer Says What They Mean Safe Route Bridge to Restless 1 "We already have coffee." Habit defense / functional claim / clean no "When does coffee work well, and when does it stop being enough?" → "When does the crew need energy most?" "That's the window I'd test, not argue about." 2 "The crew won't care." Social risk / no champion / prior failure "Has a coach or alpha tried it visibly?" → "Who does the crew watch?" "We protect the champion's credibility. Start with the alpha." 3 "Send me some info." Stall / not interested / needs social proof "What info would change a trial to yes?" → "Who else has asked that?" "I'll bring the label, a sample, and a sample Activation Standard. 10 minutes." 4 "Is it OPSS-approved?" Military buyer / procurement check / risk aversion "I won't claim certification I can't verify. Here's the label and current status." "If certification is the gate, I'll follow up when status changes." 5 "It's too expensive." Price vs. value / budget constraint / comparison "Let's look at cost per serving vs. what the crew buys now." → Calculator "One stick replaces the coffee + hydration + pre-workout stack." 6 "120mg isn't enough." High tolerance / comparison to 300mg products "Designed for a steadier use-window test, not a spike. Read the label, then let one use window decide." "More mg is not always the right fit. Test it for one shift." 7 "We use Celsius / Monster." Brand loyalty / habit / social norm "What does the crew do at hour 3?" → "When do people reach for the next thing?" "Different format: one stick in water, with the label visible." 8 "Never heard of you." Trust gap / need for credibility / risk aversion Founder story + evidence pack + retime if needed "That's fair. We were built for this market, not the grocery aisle. Here's why." Hard Claim Guardrails — What NOT to Say × "Proven to reduce errors" → ✓ "Formulated for energy + hydration; trial decides fit" × "Clinically tested" → ✓ "Ingredients with published research" × "Safe for everyone" → ✓ "Review the label with your doctor" × "Better than [competitor]" → ✓ "Different formula — here's the label" × "Guaranteed energy" → ✓ "Designed for a steadier use-window test" When to Use Honest Out: No named owner + no feedback loop + oversized ask + high social risk + no decision maker = honest out. Preserve the relationship. Code it. Move. Appendix F — Full Objection Matrix & Claim Guardrails 27 / 30

Status tells the rep whether to cite externally, hedge, or hold internal-only. If a claim is Needs confirmation , do not put it in writing to a buyer until status changes. L-theanine 240 mg per stick Cover quote; Tool 09 F1; Card #2 Founder transcript (¶117): “control that stress response with 240 milligrams of L-theanine” Verified — founder 2026-05-18 OK to say. Still confirm exact mg against current Supplement Facts panel before written quotes. ~1,000 mg electrolytes per stick Cover quote; Tool 02A; Card #2 Supplement Facts panel (Na / K / Mg sum) Internal assumption — Round figure — if buyer wants precision, send the panel. $84 wholesale / case Tool 01 Revenue Math sidebar Planning assumption (rep quota model) Internal assumption — Do not cite externally.
